item.title: [CVPR 2026] Designing Medicines Like Software: The Keynote That Made Biology Programmable
item.body:
> At CVPR 2026, Latent Labs founder Simon Kohl argued that the same generative AI reshaping computer vision is turning drug discovery from a game of chance into an act of design—typing what you want and having the machine build the molecule.

## What was announced

In his CVPR 2026 keynote, "Programmable Biology: Generative AI for Molecular Design," Simon Kohl—founder and CEO of Latent Labs and a former DeepMind scientist who worked on the Nobel Prize-winning AlphaFold2—laid out a simple but radical shift.

For decades, finding a new drug meant *searching*: sift through millions of existing molecules and hope one happens to stick to the disease target you care about. Kohl's message was that generative AI is flipping that logic. Instead of finding molecules by chance, you can now *design them from intent*—describe the job you need done, and an AI model generates a brand-new molecule, atom by atom, to fit.

He walked through Latent Labs' models:

- **Latent-X** builds proteins at the "all-atom" level—placing every atom in three-dimensional space. It designs small molecular tools like macrocyclic peptides and protein "mini-binders," replacing millions of random screening attempts with dozens of targeted designs.
- **Latent-X2** extends this to **antibodies**, the workhorse molecules behind many modern medicines. Kohl highlighted that its AI-designed antibody candidates did **not** trigger an immune reaction in human blood-donor lab tests—reported as a first for any AI-generated antibody.
- **Latent-Y** is an AI agent that takes a plain-language request and runs a whole design campaign—picking the target, designing candidates, and checking them computationally—acting either on its own or as a co-pilot.

A key point for the computer-vision audience: Kohl argued that protein design and image generation share the same core problems—generating complex structures, steering them with multiple inputs, and reasoning step by step.

## Why it matters

Drug discovery is famously slow, expensive, and failure-prone. The traditional approach can take years of trial-and-error just to nudge a molecule toward the right properties—how tightly it binds, how stable it is, whether the body tolerates it.

If those properties can instead be specified up front and generated on the first pass, the economics change. Fewer dead ends, faster timelines, and the ability to go after targets that were previously too hard. The immunogenicity result matters especially, because an antibody that provokes the immune system is a non-starter in the clinic—getting that right computationally removes one of the field's biggest late-stage risks.

For everyday readers, the bigger idea is the framing: biology is becoming **programmable**. Just as software let us write instructions for computers, generative models may let scientists "write" instructions for molecules—shifting biology from a science of what we can discover to an engineering discipline of what we can build.

## An easy way to understand it

Think of the difference between shopping and tailoring.

Old-school drug discovery is like walking into a warehouse with millions of off-the-rack coats, trying each one, and hoping something fits. It works, but it is slow and mostly luck.

Generative molecular design is like a master tailor who takes your measurements and sews a coat to fit from scratch. You state what you need—the target it must grip, the shape, the comfort—and the design is built to order. Latent-Y is like handing that tailor a written note instead of standing for a fitting: "make me a coat for this job," and it handles the rest.

## Three-line summary

1. Simon Kohl's CVPR 2026 keynote reframed drug discovery from searching for molecules to designing them from scratch with generative AI.
2. Latent Labs' models design proteins atom-by-atom, including antibodies that reportedly avoided an immune reaction in human blood tests—a claimed first—plus an agent that runs campaigns from a text prompt.
3. The vision is "programmable biology": specifying a molecule's desired properties up front instead of engineering them over years.

## Today's terms

- **Generative AI**: Models that create new content—here, entirely new molecules—rather than just recognizing or sorting existing ones.
- **All-atom generative model**: An AI that designs a molecule by placing every individual atom in 3D space, not just sketching a rough shape.
- **Antibody**: A Y-shaped protein the immune system uses to latch onto specific targets; many modern drugs are engineered antibodies.
- **De novo design**: Creating a molecule from scratch to meet a goal, instead of finding one that already exists.
- **Immunogenicity**: Whether a molecule provokes an unwanted immune reaction in the body—a make-or-break property for medicines.
